seo.page_title: Nissan Leaf 2022 import from Japan to NZ
seo.meta_description: Import a 2022 Nissan Leaf from Japan to NZ with clear costs, battery health checks, real-world range in km and support through shipping and entry standards.
seo.h1_tag: Import a 2022 Nissan Leaf to NZ
seo.og_title: Nissan Leaf 2022 – import direct from Japan to NZ
seo.og_description: Looking at a 2022 Nissan Leaf? We help you source the right car in Japan, check battery health, explain real-world range in km and manage shipping and NZ entry standards.

blocks.hero_h1: Import a 2022 Nissan Leaf to NZ
blocks.hero_subtitle: Bring a 2022 Nissan Leaf in from Japan with clear pricing, a focus on battery health and a range that works for real NZ driving. Most 40 kWh cars suit daily use, while 62 kWh Leaf e+ models cover longer runs.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: The 2022 Nissan Leaf is a practical way to go electric for commuting, school runs and weekend trips without using a non-EV car at all. The 40 kWh version typically gives around 220–240 km of mixed NZ driving, while the 62 kWh Leaf e+ can usually manage 320–340 km if the battery is in good shape. Later-model Leafs add stronger safety tech and more range than early cars, so for many households this replaces a main car, not just a city runabout.

savings_block.headline: What you can save vs buying a 2022 Leaf on a NZ yard
savings_block.body: Importing a 2022 Nissan Leaf from Japan often works out cheaper than buying similar stock already on a NZ yard, especially if you’re after a specific trim or lower mileage. For most buyers, landed cost for a 2022 Leaf ends up in a broad band rather than one fixed number, typically shaped by battery size, auction price and mileage. Your landed cost includes the auction price, our fee, shipping, insurance, and NZ entry certification and compliance, and we lay out the numbers up front so you can compare against local asking prices before you commit.

cta_form.headline: Find your 2022 Nissan Leaf — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: Take a look at the 2022 Leaf’s cabin, large screen and simple drive selector — this is what your daily EV drive will actually feel like.
blocks.main_usp_deep: A 2022 Nissan Leaf gives you modern EV safety and comfort with low running costs. The standard 40 kWh battery is usually good for about 220–240 km of real-world NZ driving, while the 62 kWh Leaf e+ can often deliver 320–340 km if the battery health is strong. Both use an electric motor around 110–160 kW depending on trim, which is plenty for motorway on-ramps and passing. We focus on battery State of Health, auction grade and charging history when shortlisting cars, so you can see how the pack has been treated before you commit.

buyer_persona.headline: Is this car right for you?
buyer_persona.body: Best suited for: households that want to cut running costs, commuters with a regular daily route up to around 80–120 km a day, and families who do most of their driving in and around town with the odd regional trip. If you’re happy to plan the occasional 30–40 minute fast-charge stop on longer runs and want a quiet, simple EV to live with, a 2022 Leaf is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: We’re an NZ-based team that’s been importing cars from Japan since 2009, including many Nissan Leafs. We give you direct access to Japanese auctions, explain the grades in plain language and share the full auction sheets and photos before you decide to bid. Our pricing shows the full landed picture, from auction hammer price through to shipping and NZ entry certification and compliance. You stay in control of the decisions while we handle the legwork and paperwork.

car_specs_benefits.benefit: Suited to shorter commutes and city use, with around 220–240 km of real-world range when the battery is healthy and easy overnight charging at home.
Gives a noticeably longer usable range, usually around 320–340 km in mixed NZ driving, making regional trips and less frequent charging more realistic.
Delivers smooth, instant response for merging, passing and hills without needing to think about gears.
No non-EV stops, fewer moving parts and simpler servicing, which typically lowers day-to-day running costs.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Hatchback body with folding rear seats
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Easy to load groceries, prams or sports gear, and still compact enough to park in tight city spaces.

cta_mid_page.headline: Start a 2022 Leaf search today
cta_mid_page.subtext: Tell us the battery size, budget and features you want, and we’ll come back with real auction options from Japan.

models_comparison.intro: Here’s how the main 2022 Nissan Leaf variants differ when you’re importing from Japan.

rows.notes: For a 2022 Leaf you’re safely within current NZ ESC and safety rules, and these cars typically meet NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no major modifications are noted.
Most 2022 Leafs use the 40 kWh battery, while the Leaf e+ uses a 62 kWh pack with more real-world range. Which one works best depends on your daily distance and how often you want to fast-charge.
Higher trims in Japan can add features like upgraded audio, more driver-assist tech and different interior finishes. We can target specific trims once you tell us what matters to you.

Being a full EV, the 2022 Leaf avoids road-user charges for now but may attract different fees in future. Based on auction documentation, this model usually meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted.

Standard 40 kWh 2022 Leafs are more common at auction, so there’s usually more choice and a wider price spread. The 62 kWh Leaf e+ is less common, which can mean waiting a bit longer for the right car.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief and give you a cost breakdown before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For EVs like the Leaf, we pay close attention to reported battery health, usage notes and any mention of fast-charging patterns on each shortlisted car.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet and our recommendation. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common trims or the 62 kWh Leaf e+, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing a 2022 Leaf is straightforward, but it helps to understand the main risks and how we handle them.

items.risk: Worrying about hidden issues with the battery or past use in Japan.
items.solution: We focus on battery health indicators, auction grades and usage notes, and share the full documentation and photos before you decide to bid. You can also budget for a battery check in NZ once the car arrives.

items.risk: Concern that extra costs will appear after you’ve started the process.
items.solution: We outline the expected landed cost up front, including auction price estimate, our fee, shipping, insurance and NZ entry certification and compliance. If anything changes, we explain it before you commit further.

items.risk: Not being sure what an auction grade really means for condition.
items.solution: We translate the auction sheet into plain language, point out any marks or notes that matter, and compare similar cars so you can see how grade, mileage and price line up.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: You tell us the year, battery size, budget and features you want in a 2022 Leaf, and we watch the Japanese auctions for cars that match. We’ll send you options with full documentation, landed cost estimates and our view on battery health so you can choose. When you’re ready, we handle bidding, shipping and the NZ side while you wait for the car to arrive.

faq_block.q: What real-world range can I expect from a 2022 Nissan Leaf in NZ?
faq_block.a: It depends on battery size and health. The 40 kWh version is typically good for about 220–240 km of mixed NZ driving, while the 62 kWh Leaf e+ usually manages around 320–340 km if the battery is in good shape.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import a 2022 Leaf from Japan?
faq_block.a: Total cost varies with battery size, trim, mileage and auction price. Your landed cost includes auction price, our fee, shipping, insurance, and NZ entry certification and compliance.

faq_block.q: Will a 2022 Leaf meet NZ entry standards?
faq_block.a: A 2022 Leaf usually meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final approval is confirmed after inspection in NZ.

faq_block.q: How long does it take from bidding to driving my Leaf in NZ?
faq_block.a: Shipping to NZ is typically 2–4 weeks, then allow time for clearance, entry certification and registration. Many buyers see their car in 6–10 weeks.

faq_block.q: What auction grade should I look for on a 2022 Leaf?
faq_block.a: Higher grades mean tidier condition, but mid-grade cars can offer good value. We explain grades and help you balance grade, mileage and budget.

footer_cta.headline: Ready to look for a 2022 Nissan Leaf?
footer_cta.subtext: Tell us what you’re after and we’ll start watching the Japanese auctions, then come back with real options, pricing and battery health details.

subscription_block.headline: Stay in the loop
subscription_block.subtext: Get updates on Nissan Leaf auctions, EV import tips and example landed prices direct to your inbox.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is an NZ-based import service that’s been helping buyers bring cars in from Japan since 2009. We focus on matching people with the right vehicle, sharing full auction documentation and explaining the process in plain language. EVs like the Nissan Leaf are a growing part of what we handle every week.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Your landed cost for a 2022 Nissan Leaf covers getting the car from a Japanese auction to legal on NZ roads.

rows.notes: The hammer price you pay for the specific 2022 Leaf you choose, based on battery size, trim and condition.
Transport from the Japanese port to NZ and cover for the car while it’s on the water.
Inspection, paperwork and any work needed so the Leaf can be registered and driven on NZ roads.

auction_checklist.check: Battery SOH and any notes on charging history
auction_checklist.why: This shapes real-world range and long-term ownership costs more than minor cosmetic wear.
Gives a clear picture of cosmetic condition and any recorded repairs before you bid.
Helps you judge how hard the car has been used and whether the mileage matches the grade and price.
Confirms trim level, seat condition and features like heated seats or driver-assist systems.
Important for long-term durability and NZ entry standards, especially if the car has lived in snowy regions of Japan.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: You regularly tow or carry very heavy loads, where a different type of vehicle would be more suitable.
You often drive very long distances in one hit and don’t want to plan any fast-charge stops.
You want a very low upfront price and aren’t comfortable with the idea of importing and waiting for the right car.

alternatives_block.intro: If a 2022 Leaf doesn’t quite match your range or space needs, it can be worth looking at other EVs.

rows.better_if: You’re happy with a bit less range and want to keep the upfront cost down.
rows.tradeoff: Lower purchase price but usually more battery wear and older safety tech.

battery_health_block.headline: Battery health on a 2022 Nissan Leaf
battery_health_block.body: For a 2022 Leaf, the battery is still relatively new, but there can already be differences in State of Health depending on how the car was used and charged in Japan. On a good example, you’d expect SOH to still be in the high 80s to mid 90s percent range, which helps the 40 kWh cars stay near 220–240 km and the 62 kWh Leaf e+ closer to 320–340 km in mixed NZ use. We look for consistent SOH readings, moderate mileage and no signs of heavy rapid-charging use, then share that information with you before you decide to bid. Once the car lands in NZ, you can choose to have an independent battery scan done for extra peace of mind.
checklist: Confirm reported SOH and compare with age and mileage
Look for notes about frequent fast-charging or taxi/ride-share use
Check for any battery-related warning lights in auction photos
Favour cars with regular use over long periods of storage
Budget for a battery health check in NZ after arrival

charging_block.headline: Charging a 2022 Leaf in NZ
charging_block.home_charging: Most 2022 Leaf owners in NZ charge at home overnight using a dedicated wall unit, which is kinder to the wiring and usually faster than a standard socket. You can set charging times to line up with cheaper night rates if your power company offers them.
charging_block.public_charging: On the road, the Leaf can use public AC chargers for top-ups and DC fast chargers for quicker stops on longer trips. How often you’ll need public charging depends on your daily distance and whether you choose the 40 kWh or 62 kWh battery.
caveats: If you live in an apartment or have no off-street parking, think through how and where you’ll charge before you commit.
Fast-charging is handy but relying on it heavily can affect long-term battery health, so it’s best as an occasional tool rather than your only charging method.
Public charger availability can vary by region, so check your local network coverage if you often drive outside main centres.

blocks.ev_running_costs: Running a 2022 Nissan Leaf in NZ typically costs less per kilometre than a similar-sized non-EV car, especially if you can charge at home on a good power plan. Many owners see energy costs in the ballpark of 3–5 cents per km from home charging, depending on their tariff. There’s no oil to change and fewer moving parts, so servicing is more about checks, cabin filters and brake fluid than major mechanical work, though you’ll still need tyres, WOFs and registration.
blocks.range_real_world: Real-world range for a 2022 Leaf depends on battery size, driving style, terrain and weather. The 40 kWh cars are typically good for about 220–240 km of mixed NZ driving when the battery is healthy, which covers most commutes and school runs with a buffer. The 62 kWh Leaf e+ usually stretches that to around 320–340 km, giving more headroom for regional trips and days with lots of driving, though hills, high speeds and winter use will still trim those numbers a bit.
blocks.model_deep_dive: The 2022 Nissan Leaf sits in a useful spot where you get a modern EV platform, updated safety tech and fresher interiors, without paying new-car money. Japanese domestic market cars often have extra comfort or tech features compared with NZ-new examples, and there’s a good spread of mileage and trim levels at auction. When we search, we match you to specific cars that line up with your battery size preference, colour, interior and budget, then walk you through the pros and cons of each option, including expected range in km based on SOH.
blocks.trim_comparison: For 2022, the main choice is between standard 40 kWh Leafs and the higher-capacity 62 kWh Leaf e+. Within those, Japanese trims can add features like upgraded audio, different wheels, heated seats and extra driver-assist systems. Rather than guessing from badges alone, we use the auction sheet and photos to confirm what each car actually has, and talk through how the 40 kWh’s roughly 220–240 km range compares with the 62 kWh’s usual 320–340 km in day-to-day NZ use.
blocks.not_for_you: A 2022 Leaf is not ideal if you need to tow regularly, drive very long distances without stopping, or don’t have a practical way to charge at home or work. If you often do 400 km-plus days with limited charging on the way, another EV with a larger pack or a different type of vehicle suits that use case better.
